text: The surgeon opens the left retroperitoneal space, exposing the ureter and internal iliac artery. The pararectal and pararesical fossae are opened to clearly visualize the medial branch of the internal iliac artery, identified as the left uterine artery. Surrounding tissue is carefully removed for safe and proximal control of the uterine artery.
The surgeon tracks the uterine artery, preparing to place a clip just below it. The uterine artery is clearly visible, and the assistant provides medial traction on the left ureter.

The surgeon opens the peritoneum between the right infundibulopelvic ligament and the round ligament, accessing the avascular retroperitoneal space. Classical landmarks are identified, including the ureter medially and the internal iliac artery laterally. Both the pararectal and paravesical spaces are developed, revealing two uterine arteries, which are dissected for improved vascular control.

The surgeon identifies the second uterine artery and applies two clips for vascular control, placing the first clip on the downward artery and the second clip on the cranial artery to ensure a bloodless laparoscopic myomectomy.

The uterus is well devascularized, revealing two visible myomas. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make a longitudinal incision between the posterior myomas, reaching the myoma capsule. The assistants and surgeon apply lateral traction on the uterine serosa while the harmonic scalpel cuts through the tissue. Upon reaching the myoma capsule, the avascular step of the procedure begins, involving traction on the left myoma to facilitate its removal.

The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction to identify the correct dissecting plane, then cuts the vascular attachments surrounding the myoma to facilitate a fast and bloodless laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel on the uterine serosa to minimize lateral tissue damage compared to classical polymyomectomy techniques. Vascular bridges are identified and coagulated with the harmonic scalpel, allowing for rapid removal of the second myoma.
Both myomas are placed in the Douglas sponge, visible within the surgical field. The surgeon proceeds without performing bipolar hemostasis on the myoma bed.
The surgeon rapidly places stitches using a hemostatic technique, taking a large bite of the uterine serosa to ensure effective hemostasis.

The surgeon uses a 14mm Vicryl needle to perform an intracorporeal knotting technique, aiming to invaginate healthy tissue into the myoma bed. The interrupted sutures are placed to close the myoma bed with healthy serosa, reducing the risk of post-operative hematoma.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of taking a large bite of healthy myometrial tissue far from the edges of the myoma, evaginating the myometrium into the myoma bed to create a strong scar suitable for vaginal delivery.

The surgeon demonstrates a bloodless myomectomy procedure, avoiding opening the endometrium to minimize the risk of postoperative synechiae. A double flat knot is tied using intracorporeal knotting technique, effectively invaginating the tissue for anatomical reconstruction.

The surgeon continues the myomectomy procedure, ensuring stitches are placed away from the proximal part of the core tubes in the corneal area. A double flat knot is tied, followed by invagination, demonstrating effective anatomical reconstruction. The final knot is placed in the upper part of the hystereotomy.

The surgeon ties the last knot, achieving invagination of the tissue with visible blanching at the edges, indicating well-tied knots. The hystereotomy is now closed, and the procedure progresses to morcellation using an endobag.

The surgeon performs morcellation using a 50mm diameter morcellator set at 700 revolutions per minute within an endobag to minimize the risk of postoperative leiomyomatosis and leiomyosarcoma. The endobag is then removed to inspect and control the operative field.
The surgeon closes the 15mm incision site where the morcellator was inserted, ensuring the final aspect of the laparoscopic myomectomy is complete.
